quiz_questions.question_text: The debt avalanche method prioritizes paying off:

options.text: The oldest debt first
The newest debt first
The smallest balance first
The highest interest rate first

quiz_questions.explanation: The debt avalanche method targets the highest interest rate debt first while making minimum payments on all others. This is the mathematically optimal approach that minimizes total interest paid.

quiz_questions.question_text: The debt snowball method prioritizes:

options.text: The highest interest rate first
All debts equally
The largest balance first
The smallest balance first to build psychological momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: The snowball method focuses on eliminating the smallest balance first, creating quick wins that motivate continued effort. While it costs more in interest than the avalanche, it has a higher success rate due to psychological benefits.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which method costs less in total interest: avalanche or snowball?

options.text: They always cost the same amount
Avalanche costs less because targeting highest rates first minimizes total interest
It depends entirely on the balances
Snowball always costs less

quiz_questions.explanation: The avalanche method always results in less total interest paid because it eliminates the most expensive debt first. The snowball may cost more in interest but is often more effective behaviorally.

quiz_questions.question_text: Consolidating $25,000 from 20% to 10% saves approximately _____ per year in interest.
quiz_questions.explanation: At 20%: $25,000 x 0.20 = $5,000/year. At 10%: $25,000 x 0.10 = $2,500/year. The rate reduction saves approximately $2,500 annually in interest charges.

quiz_questions.question_text: Debt consolidation is only effective if:

options.text: You always consolidate regardless of terms
The new loan has a longer repayment term
You make only minimum payments on the new loan
You avoid accumulating new debt on the now-empty credit cards

quiz_questions.explanation: Consolidation reduces rates and simplifies payments, but if borrowers run up new balances on freed-up credit cards, they end up with more total debt than before — a common and dangerous trap.

quiz_questions.question_text: A DTI ratio above _____ is considered risky by most lenders.

options.text: 43% (the Qualified Mortgage threshold; many lenders prefer below 36%)

quiz_questions.explanation: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau sets 43% DTI as the maximum for Qualified Mortgages. Most financial advisors recommend keeping total DTI below 36%, with no more than 28% allocated to housing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Balance transfer cards with 0% introductory APR are most useful when:

options.text: You make only minimum payments during the promo period
You can pay off the entire balance before the promotional period ends
You keep transferring between cards indefinitely
You transfer the balance and continue using the old card

quiz_questions.explanation: The 0% introductory APR is a powerful tool only if you pay off the balance before the promo ends. Once the rate jumps (often to 20%+), any remaining balance becomes very expensive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chapter 7 bankruptcy:

options.text: Has no consequences and eliminates all debt
Only applies to businesses, not individuals
Discharges most unsecured debt but has severe consequences including credit impact for 10 years
Is always the best option for anyone with debt

quiz_questions.explanation: Chapter 7 liquidation bankruptcy discharges most unsecured debts but stays on your credit report for 10 years, may require surrendering certain assets, and does not discharge student loans or tax debts in most cases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Creating an emergency fund while paying off debt is important because:

options.text: Without one, unexpected expenses go on credit cards, creating new debt and a vicious cycle
It delays debt payoff, which is always better
Emergency funds earn enough interest to offset debt
Only wealthy people need emergency funds

quiz_questions.explanation: A small emergency fund (typically $1,000-$2,000) prevents unexpected expenses from being charged to credit cards, which would undermine debt payoff progress and create a cycle of re-accumulating debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: A debt management plan (DMP) through a nonprofit credit counselor:

options.text: Is always a scam
Negotiates lower rates and consolidates payments over 3-5 years; less damaging than bankruptcy
Eliminates all debt instantly
Requires filing for bankruptcy first

quiz_questions.explanation: A DMP through a reputable nonprofit counselor negotiates reduced interest rates with creditors, combines multiple payments into one, and typically takes 3-5 years to complete. It is far less damaging to credit than bankruptcy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'debt avalanche' method?

options.text: A repayment strategy that directs all extra payments to the debt with the highest interest rate first
Filing for bankruptcy to eliminate all debts at once
A strategy of consolidating all debts into a single loan
Making equal extra payments to all debts simultaneously

quiz_questions.explanation: The debt avalanche strategy involves making minimum payments on all debts except the one with the highest interest rate, which receives all available extra funds. Once that debt is paid off, the freed-up money rolls to the next highest rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'debt-to-income ratio' (DTI) mean?

options.text: The percentage of gross monthly income that goes toward monthly debt payments
The interest rate charged on the largest debt
The ratio of credit card balances to credit limits
The total amount of debt divided by the total value of assets

quiz_questions.explanation: DTI = Total Monthly Debt Payments / Gross Monthly Income. It measures how much of your income is committed to debt obligations and is a key metric lenders use to assess borrowing capacity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'debt consolidation' mean?

options.text: Transferring debt from one credit card to another
Refusing to pay any debts until they are reduced
Combining multiple debts into a single loan, ideally at a lower interest rate, to simplify payments
Declaring bankruptcy to eliminate all debts

quiz_questions.explanation: Debt consolidation merges multiple debts (credit cards, loans) into one new loan with a single monthly payment and, ideally, a lower interest rate. Success depends on not accumulating new debt on freed-up credit lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'lifestyle inflation' (lifestyle creep)?

options.text: The increase in prices of goods and services over time
The tendency to increase spending as income rises, preventing financial progress despite earning more
The cost of living adjustment applied to salaries
A tax increase that reduces take-home pay

quiz_questions.explanation: Lifestyle inflation occurs when people upgrade their spending habits every time their income increases, channeling raises into upgraded expenses rather than debt payoff or savings, keeping them in the same financial position.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'debt settlement' mean?

options.text: Combining all debts into a single monthly payment
Filing for Chapter 13 bankruptcy protection
Paying off debt in full according to the original terms
Negotiating with creditors to accept a lump-sum payment that is less than the total amount owed

quiz_questions.explanation: Debt settlement involves negotiating with creditors to accept less than the full balance owed. While it can reduce total debt, it severely damages credit scores, may trigger tax liability on forgiven amounts, and creditors are not obligated to negotiate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'Chapter 13 bankruptcy'?

options.text: A type of bankruptcy available only to corporations
A bankruptcy that liquidates all assets to pay creditors
A voluntary credit counseling program with no legal proceedings
A reorganization plan where the debtor keeps assets and repays all or part of debts over 3-5 years under court supervision

quiz_questions.explanation: Chapter 13 allows individuals to keep their property while repaying debts through a court-approved 3-5 year plan. It stays on credit reports for 7 years (vs. 10 for Chapter 7) and is suited for those with regular income.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'snowball effect' mean in debt repayment?

options.text: A seasonal pattern of spending more during winter holidays
Rolling over credit card balances month to month
Debt growing larger over time due to compound interest
The acceleration that occurs as each paid-off debt frees up money to apply to the next, creating increasingly larger payments

quiz_questions.explanation: As each debt is eliminated, the amount previously devoted to it becomes available for the next debt. The payment grows like a rolling snowball, accelerating payoff of each subsequent debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'debt fatigue'?

options.text: A lender's refusal to extend additional credit
The decreasing interest rate on long-term loans
The financial impact of accumulating too much debt
The emotional exhaustion and loss of motivation that occurs during long-term debt repayment, often leading to giving up

quiz_questions.explanation: Debt fatigue is a psychological phenomenon where borrowers become discouraged by slow progress and abandon their repayment plan. The snowball method's quick wins help combat this by providing early motivation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'hardship program' offered by creditors?

options.text: A government-mandated debt forgiveness program
A temporary arrangement where creditors may reduce interest rates, waive fees, or lower payments for borrowers facing financial difficulty
A program that increases interest rates for high-risk borrowers
A savings account designed for emergency situations

quiz_questions.explanation: Many creditors offer hardship programs for borrowers experiencing job loss, medical emergencies, or other financial difficulties. These may temporarily reduce rates or payments but typically require documentation of hardship.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'good debt vs. bad debt' mean?

options.text: All debt is equally harmful to your financial health
Good debt finances assets that appreciate or increase earning potential (education, real estate); bad debt finances depreciating consumption (credit cards, high-interest consumer loans)
Good debt is from banks; bad debt is from credit unions
Good debt has a fixed rate; bad debt has a variable rate

quiz_questions.explanation: Good debt typically has lower rates and finances assets that build wealth (mortgage, student loans for high-ROI degrees). Bad debt carries high rates and funds consumption that loses value, like credit card purchases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Maria has three debts: a $5,000 credit card at 22%, a $20,000 student loan at 6%, and a $15,000 car loan at 5%. She has $300/month extra beyond minimums. Using the avalanche method, she should direct the extra $300 to:

options.text: The credit card at 22% because it has the highest interest rate
Split equally: $100 to each debt
The student loan because it has the largest balance
The car loan because it has the lowest rate

quiz_questions.explanation: The avalanche method targets the highest interest rate first. The credit card at 22% is the most expensive debt, so all $300 extra (plus the $125 minimum) goes there first. This minimizes total interest paid across all three debts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using Maria's scenario above, the credit card minimum is $125 and she adds $300 extra for $425/month total. At $425/month on $5,000 at 22%, the card is paid off in approximately:

options.text: 6 months
~14 months
24 months
36 months

quiz_questions.explanation: At $425/month on a $5,000 balance at 22%, the card is eliminated in approximately 14 months. Without the extra payments, minimum-only payments would take years and cost far more in interest.

quiz_questions.question_text: The total cost of carrying $10,000 in credit card debt at 20% for 10 years making only minimum payments is approximately:

options.text: ~$20,000+ (you may pay more in interest than the original balance)

quiz_questions.explanation: At 20% APR with minimum payments (often 2% of balance), the declining minimum means most of each payment goes to interest. Over a decade, total payments can exceed $20,000, meaning you paid more in interest than the original debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: Carlos has $8,000 on a credit card at 24% APR. A balance transfer card offers 0% APR for 15 months with a 3% transfer fee ($240). If Carlos pays $550/month, can he pay it off before the promo ends and how much does he save?

options.text: Yes, he pays it off in about 15 months and saves roughly $1,800 in interest (minus the $240 fee, net savings ~$1,560)
He should keep the original card since the transfer fee costs more
No, he cannot pay it off in 15 months
Yes, but the transfer fee eliminates all savings

quiz_questions.explanation: $8,240 (including fee) / $550 = ~15 months. Without the transfer, 24% APR on $8,000 over 15 months generates approximately $1,800 in interest. After the $240 fee, net savings are about $1,560.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple earns $7,000/month gross and has the following monthly debt payments: mortgage $1,800, car loan $400, student loans $300, credit card minimums $200. Their DTI ratio is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Total monthly debt payments: $1,800 + $400 + $300 + $200 = $2,700. DTI = $2,700 / $7,000 = 38.6%. This is below the 43% QM threshold but above the preferred 36% level, suggesting they should reduce non-housing debt.

quiz_questions.question_text: Amy has three debts: Card A ($2,000 at 18%), Card B ($500 at 22%), and a personal loan ($6,000 at 9%). Using the snowball method, she pays them in which order?

options.text: Card B ($500), Card A ($2,000), personal loan ($6,000) — smallest balance first
Card B ($22%), Card A (18%), personal loan (9%) — highest rate first
All three simultaneously with equal extra payments
Personal loan first because it has the lowest rate

quiz_questions.explanation: The snowball method orders debts by balance from smallest to largest regardless of interest rate. Card B ($500) is paid first for a quick win, then Card A ($2,000), then the personal loan ($6,000).

quiz_questions.question_text: David consolidated $30,000 in credit card debt (average 22% APR) into a personal loan at 10% for 5 years. His monthly payment is $637. After 2 years of on-time payments, he has accumulated $8,000 in new credit card debt. What went wrong?

options.text: The consolidation loan rate was too high
He should have filed for bankruptcy instead
He kept spending on the freed-up credit cards, now owing the consolidation loan plus new card debt — worse than before
Consolidation never works for anyone

quiz_questions.explanation: David fell into the consolidation trap: he freed up credit card capacity by paying them off with the loan, then accumulated $8,000 in new charges. His total debt is now the remaining consolidation loan balance plus $8,000 — more than the original $30,000.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sarah earns $4,500/month and wants to follow the 50/30/20 budget rule while paying off $12,000 in credit card debt at 21%. Under this rule, she allocates 20% ($900) to debt payoff and savings. If she dedicates $750/month to the debt and $150 to an emergency fund, approximately how long until the card is paid off?

options.text: ~12 months
~24 months
~36 months
~18 months

quiz_questions.explanation: At $750/month on $12,000 at 21% APR (1.75%/month), approximately 18 months are needed to pay off the balance. The $150/month emergency fund builds a $2,700 safety net during this period, preventing setbacks.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tom and Lisa have $45,000 in combined debt: $5,000 credit card at 24%, $15,000 car loan at 6%, and $25,000 student loan at 5%. Using the avalanche method with $500/month extra, they pay off the credit card in about 12 months. The freed-up $500 + $150 card minimum ($650 extra) now goes to which debt?

options.text: The student loan because it has the largest balance
Split between both remaining debts equally
The car loan at 6% because it is the next highest interest rate
Into savings since the highest-rate debt is gone

quiz_questions.explanation: Following the avalanche method, after eliminating the 24% credit card, the $650 in freed-up funds rolls to the car loan at 6% (the next highest rate). The student loan at 5% continues with minimum payments until the car is paid off.

quiz_questions.question_text: A recent graduate earning $48,000/year has $30,000 in student loans (5%), $3,000 in credit card debt (19%), and $10,000 in a car loan (4.5%). Monthly minimums total $650. She has $200/month extra and is deciding between avalanche and snowball. Using avalanche, she targets the credit card first. Using snowball, she also targets the credit card first (smallest balance). In this case, both methods:

options.text: Should both be ignored in favor of consolidation
Produce different results because the methods are always different
Will both recommend targeting the car loan first
Produce the same result because the smallest balance also has the highest rate

quiz_questions.explanation: When the debt with the highest interest rate also has the smallest balance, the avalanche and snowball methods agree on the payoff order. The $3,000 credit card at 19% is both the smallest balance and highest rate, so both methods target it first.
